metadata.title: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = quantum / wave / pattern; CRIMSON = single dot / localized detection
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Bragg/Davisson-Germer angle calc, no which-path/decoherence, no biprism optics. Wonder register: wave-function-through-both-slits from first principles, no drills.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Send electrons through a double slit one at a time — never two in the machine at once. An interference pattern still assembles itself, dot by dot. That sentence should stop you cold.

card.copy: Why One Electron Builds an Interference Pattern by Itself

beats.narration_text: Start with water waves through two slits. Classic interference — two wavefronts spreading, overlapping, reinforcing where they're in step, canceling where they're out of step. Bright and dark bands appear.

production_viz.label: water waves through two slits — interference pattern

beats.narration_text: Two waves interfering requires two things in the machine simultaneously. If only one electron is present at a time — nothing to interfere with — classical physics predicts a featureless smear. Not fringes. Never fringes.

card.copy: One electron at a time: no partner to interfere with. Classical predicts a smear. The experiment shows fringes. Why?

beats.narration_text: Each electron lands as a single dot. A click, a spot, one definite place. That part is perfectly particle-like — no smear, no spread.

production_viz.label: single electron dots landing one at a time

beats.narration_text: But the wave function of that single electron passed through both slits at once. It spread from each opening, traveled both paths simultaneously, and interfered with itself on the far side.

production_viz.label: single electron wave function through both slits simultaneously

beats.narration_text: Where the two wavefronts reinforce, the probability density is high. Where they cancel, it's nearly zero. Each electron has no choice but to follow this probability map — dot by dot, the fringes accumulate.

production_viz.label: probability density high at bright fringes, zero at dark

beats.narration_text: As electrons accumulate — ten, a hundred, thousands — the dot pattern fills in. The histogram of where they land matches the wave interference prediction exactly. The pattern was in each single electron all along.
This is the de Broglie wavelength at work: lambda equals h over p. The electron's momentum sets its wavelength — and that wavelength determines the fringe spacing, just as it would for light.

production_viz.label: λ = h/p — momentum sets wavelength, wavelength sets fringe spacing

beats.narration_text: Electrons at 54 electron-volts carry a de Broglie wavelength of about 0.17 nanometers — roughly the spacing between atoms in a crystal. That wavelength directly sets the fringe spacing on the detector. The numbers match. The experiment agrees.

production_viz.label: 54 eV electrons: λ ≈ 0.17 nm, fringe spacing measurable

beats.narration_text: The electron lands in one definite spot. But its wave function traveled both paths simultaneously. The probability of each landing spot came from the wave — and that wave was the whole electron, undivided, all at once.

card.copy: One electron. Both slits. One dot. The wave was the whole electron.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
